title: Job 22
subtitle: Eliphaz’s Third Speech: Direct Accusation

heb.gloss: to take a pledge / to bind
heb.paragraph: "You demanded security (ḥābal) from your relatives for no reason; you stripped people of their clothing, leaving them naked" (v.6). Eliphaz invents specific sins. Having failed to convince Job through theology, he fabricates charges: exploitation of the poor, withholding food and water, mistreating widows and orphans. None of this is supported by the narrative.

refs.note: "Share your food with the hungry" — the social justice standard Eliphaz accuses Job of violating.
"I was hungry and you gave me nothing to eat" — Jesus’ description of judgment for social neglect — the charges Eliphaz invents.

notes.note: "Can a man be of benefit to God? Can even a wise person benefit him?" Eliphaz’s opening: human righteousness has no value to God. The implication: God gains nothing from punishing or rewarding. Therefore Job’s suffering must have a cause in Job.

"Is not your wickedness great? Are not your sins endless? You stripped people naked, gave no water to the weary, withheld food from the hungry, sent widows away empty-handed, broke the arms of the fatherless." Fabricated charges. Every one contradicts the prologue.
"That is why snares are all around you, why sudden peril terrifies you." Eliphaz completes the circle: these invented sins explain Job’s suffering. The diagnosis is total fiction.
Calvin: Eliphaz has no evidence for these charges. He deduces them from Job’s suffering: if he suffers this much, he must have sinned this specifically. The reasoning is circular and cruel.
Calvin: “are not your sins endless?” — Eliphaz has condemned his friend on the basis of a theological system, not evidence. This is the danger of all theological systems applied without grace.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: ḥābal means to take a pledge as security for a debt. Eliphaz accuses Job of exploiting his relatives through predatory lending.
The NET: "you broke the arms of the fatherless" is a metaphor for removing their only support. Eliphaz accuses Job of the most vulnerable forms of oppression.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: the fabricated charges are Eliphaz’s most desperate move. When theology fails, invent evidence. The friends’ system requires Job to be guilty; since he isn’t, they must create guilt.
Clines: “Is not your wickedness great?” The question expects “yes.” Eliphaz has moved from “perhaps you sinned” (ch 4) to “your sins are endless.” The escalation is the measure of his desperation.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: the prosecution, having lost on the evidence, resorts to fabrication. Eliphaz invents crimes that fit the punishment. This is judicial corruption — precisely what Job accused God of (9:24). Habel's form-critical analysis identifies the passage's literary type and rhetorical function within the book's larger structure. The interplay of lament, wisdom disputation, and divine speech creates a multi-voiced exploration of theodicy that resists reduction to any single theological position.

hist.context: Eliphaz abandons theological argument and resorts to invention. He accuses Job of specific social crimes (vv.6–9) that directly contradict Job’s character as established in chapters 1–2 and confirmed by God himself. This is the friends’ lowest point: when the theology fails, manufacture the evidence.

sections.header: Verses 12–30 — “Submit to God and Be at Peace”

heb.paragraph: "Submit to God and be at peace (shālōm) with him" (v.21). Eliphaz’s final appeal. The promise of shalom through submission. If Job were guilty, this would be good advice. Since he isn’t, it is a demand to confess to crimes he didn’t commit.

refs.note: "Seek the LORD while he may be found" — the same call to repentance, but in Isaiah the speaker has authority; Eliphaz does not.
"Come near to God and he will come near to you" — the NT version of Eliphaz’s counsel, valid in its context.

notes.note: "Is not God in the heights of heaven? Yet you say, ‘What does God know?’" Eliphaz misquotes Job. Job never said God doesn’t know — he said God doesn’t care.

"Submit to God and be at peace... assign your nuggets to the dust, and the Almighty will be your gold." The most seductive promise yet: trade earthly wealth for divine relationship. Beautiful — if the premise were right.

"You will pray to him, and he will hear you... he will deliver even those who are not innocent." The last line is accidentally prophetic: God WILL deliver Job, who IS innocent. Eliphaz speaks better than he knows.
Calvin: “submit to God and be at peace” is excellent advice in the right context. But telling an innocent sufferer to submit to false charges is not peace — it is injustice.
Calvin: Eliphaz unknowingly prophesies. Job WILL intercede for the friends (42:8–10), and God WILL deliver them because of Job’s prayer. The irony is complete.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "submit" (sakan) means to be familiar with, to be at peace with. Eliphaz calls for restored relationship, not mere compliance.
The NET: this verse is textually difficult. Some read "he delivers the innocent" vs "he delivers even the not-innocent." Both readings point to grace exceeding strict justice.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: the call to submit is Eliphaz’s last word in the book. He will not speak again. His entire contribution reduces to: repent. The theology never evolved.

Clines: "he will deliver even those who are not innocent" may be Eliphaz’s most truthful line — delivered accidentally. God’s grace does extend beyond the innocent. But Eliphaz means it as: even YOUR uncleanness can be pardoned if you submit.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: the prosecution’s final closing argument. Eliphaz appeals for a plea deal: confess and receive mercy. But Job has nothing to confess. The prosecution has failed. Habel's form-critical analysis identifies the passage's literary type and rhetorical function within the book's larger structure. The interplay of lament, wisdom disputation, and divine speech creates a multi-voiced exploration of theodicy that resists reduction to any single theological position.

hist.context: After the fabricated charges, Eliphaz returns to the conditional promise: repent and be restored. His closing is beautiful as theology and devastating as pastoral care. “Assign your nuggets to the dust... then the Almighty will be your gold” (vv.24–25). Eliphaz imagines a restored Job who has surrendered wealth for God. The vision is attractive. The premise is false.

rows.label: The Last Appeal — 22:12–30
rows.text: “Submit and be at peace.” Beautiful counsel, false premise. Eliphaz’s final word.

rows.label: The Last Appeal — 22:12–30
rows.text: “Submit and be at peace.” Beautiful counsel, false premise. Eliphaz’s final word.

hebtext.gloss: to take a pledge / to bind
hebtext.note: "You demanded security (ḥābal) from your relatives for no reason; you stripped people of their clothing, leaving them naked" (v.6). Eliphaz invents specific sins. Having failed to convince Job through theology, he fabricates charges: exploitation of the poor, withholding food and water, mistreating widows and orphans. None of this is supported by the narrative.

"Submit to God and be at peace (shālōm) with him" (v.21). Eliphaz’s final appeal. The promise of shalom through submission. If Job were guilty, this would be good advice. Since he isn’t, it is a demand to confess to crimes he didn’t commit.

thread.text: "Share your food with the hungry" — the social justice standard Eliphaz accuses Job of violating.
"I was hungry and you gave me nothing to eat" — Jesus’ description of judgment for social neglect — the charges Eliphaz invents.
"Seek the LORD while he may be found" — the same call to repentance, but in Isaiah the speaker has authority; Eliphaz does not.
"Come near to God and he will come near to you" — the NT version of Eliphaz’s counsel, valid in its context.

themes.note: Judgment and Justice both high: Eliphaz fabricates evidence and demands repentance. The prosecution’s last stand.

prayer_prompt: As you consider Eliphaz now directly accusing Job of specific sins he never committed, consider asking God whether you have ever fabricated evidence to preserve a theory — inventing faults in others to protect your belief that suffering is always deserved.
